description: Operational knowledge in extreme environments: deep-sea diving, high-altitude operations, and aerospace. Focus on normalization of deviance — the process by which small deviations from safe practice become accepted over time, leading to catastrophic failure. Key structural parallel with fukushima_governance: authority structures that accept risk signals as normal.
grounding_notes: Boisjoly memo is in Presidential Commission on Challenger (1986) Vol.4. Vaughan 1996 (Univ. Chicago Press) is primary sociological analysis. Columbia STS-107 report (CAIB 2003) confirms mechanism generalization.

attributes.role: Morton Thiokol senior engineer, O-ring specialist
attributes.note: Wrote memo 6 months before Challenger disaster warning of O-ring cold-weather risk. Warning was overridden by management.
Approved Challenger launch at 28°F despite documented O-ring cold-weather performance concerns

attributes.override_record: 2 dive medicine recommendations shortened in past 18 months due to weather window constraints

attributes.resilience_at_28f: significantly impaired — documented in Boisjoly memo
attributes.failure_mode: joint burn-through due to inadequate sealing at low temperature

attributes.description: Prior launches showed O-ring erosion but missions succeeded. Management progressively accepted erosion as within operational tolerance — 'if we flew before with this damage, we can fly again.'

attributes.risk_of_reduction: Elevated decompression sickness probability at 18h vs. 24h: Tanaka estimates 3.2x increased DCS risk for deep saturation dives

attributes.emergency_egress: limited — decompression required, minimum 12h under any circumstances

attributes.minimum_launch_temp_f_formal: none — NASA had no formal minimum
attributes.gap: 25°F below engineering recommendation, with no formal protocol violation because no formal minimum existed

attributes.content: Warning that O-ring cold-weather resilience data showed critical failure risk. Recommended flight suspension pending design review.
attributes.action_taken: Filed. Not escalated to NASA. Design review not conducted.

mechanisms.name: Normalization of Deviance
mechanisms.notes: Vaughan (1996): when anomalies do not immediately produce failures, they become institutionally reclassified as acceptable risk. Each successful flight with O-ring erosion made the next acceptance easier.

mechanisms.name: Low Temperature O-Ring Resilience Loss
mechanisms.notes: Well-characterized physical mechanism. At 28°F, Viton rubber loses significant resilience, impairing sealing. Boisjoly had the data.

mechanisms.name: Management Override of Engineering Recommendation
mechanisms.notes: Morton Thiokol management reversed engineer consensus after NASA management expressed displeasure. Engineers asked to 'take off their engineer hat and put on their management hat.'

decisions.question: Should Challenger STS-51-L launch on January 28, 1986 at 28°F ambient temperature?
decisions.rationale: Management at Morton Thiokol reversed prior engineer consensus under pressure from NASA. Previous successful flights with O-ring damage cited as evidence that risk was acceptable. Formal risk quantification not performed at decision time.
decisions.notes: 73 seconds after launch, Challenger disintegrated due to O-ring joint failure on the right SRB.

decisions.question: Can saturation divers complete decompression on 18-hour schedule instead of 24-hour standard due to approaching weather window?
decisions.rationale: Operations Committee approved shortened decompression citing favorable DCS history on previous shortened schedules and weather window constraint.

dissents.rationale: Cold temperature significantly reduces O-ring resilience. We have data showing erosion increases with lower temperature. At 28°F, I cannot quantify the probability of joint failure, but I can say with confidence that it is unacceptably high. We should not fly below 53°F until we understand the temperature relationship fully.

Previous successful shortened decompression schedules are not evidence of safety — they are evidence we were lucky. For 1000 fsw saturation dives, the physiological model predicts 3.2x increased DCS risk at 18h vs. 24h. The weather window is a logistical constraint; it does not change the physiology.

evidence.content_ref: Morton Thiokol O-ring temperature resilience data, 1985
evidence.notes: Data clearly showed O-ring resilience decreasing with temperature. Below 53°F, resilience curve shows non-linear decline.

evidence.content_ref: Post-flight O-ring inspection data, STS flights 1984-1985
evidence.notes: Shows O-ring erosion in prior flights, but all survived. This data was used to argue that erosion was acceptable — a misuse of survivorship bias.

evidence.content_ref: Tanaka decompression risk assessment for 1000 fsw saturation dive shortened schedule
evidence.notes: 3.2x DCS risk increase at 18h vs. 24h surface interval. Based on US Navy decompression physiology model.

evidence.content_ref: Vaughan, The Challenger Launch Decision (1996), University of Chicago Press
evidence.notes: Definitive sociological analysis of normalization of deviance mechanism in the Challenger decision

constraints.description: Minimum launch temperature must ensure O-ring sealing effectiveness
constraints.notes: NASA had no formal temperature minimum in 1986. This constraint did not exist in the governance system — a governance gap.

constraints.description: Saturation dive decompression must follow standard schedule unless medical waiver obtained
constraints.notes: Waiver process exists but is rarely invoked. Shortened schedule approved operationally without formal waiver.

bridge_candidates.claim: The normalization of O-ring erosion in NASA's Challenger program is structurally identical to the normalization of TEPCO's seawall inadequacy — both involve progressive acceptance of known risk signals under institutional pressure
bridge_candidates.structural_loss: Challenger involved a discrete launch decision; TEPCO's seawall decision was a persistent infrastructure commitment. The temporal structure differs: one decision vs. years of planning inaction. The pattern of authority-override-of-expert-dissent is structurally identical.

bridge_candidates.claim: The OR Safety Committee's acceptance of insufficient supervised cases parallels the NASA management's acceptance of O-ring erosion — both involve progressive lowering of safety thresholds under schedule pressure
bridge_candidates.structural_loss: Hospital scheduling pressure differs from launch window pressure in urgency and reversibility. But the mechanism — accepting a known risk reduction as operationally normal — is structurally parallel.
